insight: Master Bot does not know base set only (BSO) lines at all; it appears to nat conduit, which always loses except against a Master Bot blastforge opening, and even then it likely plays poorly.
author: namington
context: Discussion of whether BSO lines could be tested against Master Bot to verify community analysis.

insight: Master Bot in a base-set-only game made the move WDD float 4GGG on turn 8, which is considered extremely poor play (buying a Wall then floating 4 gold with no productive use), indicating MB has no understanding of BSO opening theory.
author: extratricky
context: extratricky tested a BSO game against Master Bot to observe its behavior after community claimed MB doesn't know BSO lines.

insight: Master Bot caused a disconnect/crash by stalling long enough that Prismata announced it lost connection to the AI thread, forcing the human player to resign; a fix was reportedly being worked on by the developer.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy reported that Master Bot's AI thread connection dropped mid-game and waiting for reconnection did not help in his case.

insight: In base set only, p2's DD DA TTD (or TDD) line is considered a win against both p1 blastforge and animus openings; TDD is generally agreed to win more decisively than TTD.
author: consensus
context: Extended community discussion analyzing base set only opening lines and player advantages.

insight: In base set only, natural animus (fastimus) for p1 is generally considered losing because it is flatly behind in tempo without a compensating tech difference; the 2-drone difference from p2's response lines likely does not make up for the tempo loss.
author: leary93
context: Community debate on whether nat animus is truly losing or merely close in BSO.

insight: A critical p2 BSO line involves holding a Rhino on turn 7 to set up an exploit on a future turn; this counter to p1 natural blastforge was not widely known and, without it, p1 was previously thought to be winning.
author: mtanzer
context: mtanzer explained why BSO is closer than most players appreciate, citing the rhino hold line as a key discovery.

insight: In base set only with Energy Matrix in the set, the game becomes a p1 win; adding Energy Matrix as the +1 unit to a base set significantly shifts the balance toward p1.
author: namington
context: Discussion of how individual +1 units shift the base set balance, using Energy Matrix as an example.

insight: Lancetooth has the same problem as Tarsier in that it gets worse as inflation (economy size / attack required) increases; Bloodrager is superior in high-inflation situations, and with a Rhino+Shiver Yeti setup, Bloodrager can be acquired the following turn.
author: leary93
context: leary93 was reviewing a replay game involving Shiver Yeti, Rhino, and inflation-sensitive attackers.

insight: Amphitheater (Ampo) with 4 Tarsiers is very strong and with 6 Tarsiers is considered nearly unbeatable; getting Tarsiers 7 and 8 before securing Ampo itself is greedy and can cost the game.
author: xujhan
context: Post-game analysis where a player was observed rushing Tarsiers beyond the Ampo threshold before securing Ampo, which was called a throw.

insight: Plexo Cell is relatively weak against a Wincer hold strategy; if pursuing Wincer, players should be cautious about relying on Plexo Cell alongside it.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of a set containing Arms Race, Wincer, Plexo Cell, and Ebbs, evaluating the best strategic approach.

insight: In a heavy blue set with Omega Splitter and Energy Matrix, the p2 recommended opening is approximately DD/DDE/DDDC/DDDB/DDDW(if absorb needed)/BBS then Omega spam starting turn 7; floating 4 gold as p2 to rush straight to Blastforge is considered by some experts preferable to buying an early Conduit in such sets.
author: shadourow
context: shadourow provided detailed opening feedback to a new player in a heavy blue Omega Splitter set.

insight: In a heavy blue Omega Splitter set, rushing Omegas without first building an Energy Matrix is risky; a Matrix allows all Omegas to be clicked safely, and if the opponent rushes Omegas too early, the correct counter is to build an Energy Matrix instead.
author: amalloy
context: Debate about the optimal p2 line in a set featuring Omega Splitter, Energy Matrix, Doomed Mech, and Pixie.

insight: When choosing where to absorb damage, absorbing on a fragile unit like Tia Threnody rather than a Doomed Wall can cost granularity or deny future absorb opportunities; generally absorb should be placed on Doomed units when possible to avoid wasting lifespan.
author: drevoed
context: Post-game nitpick discussion about absorb placement choices on fragile versus doomed units.

insight: Using a Doomed Drone as early as turn 1 instead of a regular Drone can be correct in very rushy sets, as the Doomed Drone can provide defensive value while also hitting an economic breakpoint; this is unusual but situationally justified.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy analyzed a shared replay and concluded the best p1 opening involved an unusually early Doomed Drone for both economic and defensive purposes.

insight: In a set with Gaussite Symbiote and Sentinel, it is possible to stall long enough with absorb to accumulate 26 Gauss Cannon charges, which can be a decisive game-winning threat if the opponent is not anticipating such a build.
author: swapgo
context: swapgo described a game where stalling with Sentinel absorb enabled an extreme Gauss Cannon charge accumulation.
